text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy, dissecting the broad ligament peritoneum on the posterior aspect of the uterus. After lateralizing the peritoneum, the degenerated posterior wall lower segment uterine fibroid is visually identified.

Vasopressin is infiltrated into the myometrial wall adjacent to the fibroid. A cut is made over the myometrium using cold knife scissors, revealing the degenerated fibroid. Due to the fibroid's degeneration, it is manipulated and removed using traction and counter traction with needle holders.
The cut end of the uterine musculature shows minimal bleeding due to the prior infiltration with vasopressin.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing on the posterior lower end of the fibroid, using figure-of-eight sutures with vicryl due to the challenging needle angulation. The fibroid has a significant sub-serosal component, influencing the choice of suturing technique.
The surgeon uses a T-lift device to suspend the ovaries, particularly the left ovary, preventing it from dangling and obstructing the surgical field during suturing.

The surgeon uses a 3D laparoscope to enhance depth perception while suturing a low-lying posterior uterine fibroid in the pelvis. The aim is to complete the suturing within 40 to 45 minutes after vasopressin infiltration to minimize bleeding.
The surgeon uses a MorSafe bag to morcellate and extract the fibroid from the abdominal cavity, ensuring containment during removal.
